text: {"suggestedLabel":"Cautious Cholesterol Perspective","suggestedDescription":"Emphasizes the LDL‑raising effect of dietary cholesterol from eggs and treats reassuring findings as uncertain or possibly industry‑influenced.","stances":[{"claimIndex":0,"stance":"accept","rationale":"A large egg yolk indeed contains roughly 200 mg of cholesterol."},{"claimIndex":1,"stance":"reject","rationale":"The weight of trial and mortality‑association evidence shows dietary cholesterol has a clear impact on blood cholesterol, so the claim of only a weak relation is rejected."},{"claimIndex":2,"stance":"uncertain","rationale":"While two large cohort studies found no association, the overall evidence is mixed, so we remain uncertain."},{"claimIndex":3,"stance":"uncertain","rationale":"Fats certainly influence cholesterol, but dietary cholesterol also plays a significant role; the balance is uncertain."},{"claimIndex":4,"stance":"uncertain","rationale":"The Physicians’ Health Study’s conclusions are viewed with caution given possible industry influence, making the safety claim uncertain."},{"claimIndex":5,"stance":"accept","rationale":"People struggling to control LDL should limit egg yolks and prefer egg‑white foods."},{"claimIndex":6,"stance":"accept","rationale":"Evidence shows diabetics who eat one or more eggs daily have higher heart‑disease risk."},{"claimIndex":7,"stance":"accept","rationale":"Limiting egg yolks to three per week for diabetics with heart disease aligns with the precautionary approach."},{"claimIndex":8,"stance":"reject","rationale":"Even though sugary foods are unhealthy, eggs still raise LDL and cannot be deemed a better choice without reservation."},{"claimIndex":9,"stance":"accept","rationale":"A steel‑cut oat bowl with nuts and berries is a superior heart‑healthy breakfast compared with an egg‑centric one."},{"claimIndex":10,"stance":"accept","rationale":"Plant‑based proteins consistently show lower cardiovascular and overall mortality than eggs or red meat."},{"claimIndex":11,"stance":"accept","rationale":"Moderate to low egg intake, with an emphasis on plant proteins, fits the cautious dietary cholesterol stance."},{"claimIndex":12,"stance":"accept","rationale":"Eggs typically remain safe for four to six weeks past the sell‑by date when refrigerated."},{"claimIndex":13,"stance":"accept","rationale":"Cooking eggs until both whites and yolks are solidified helps prevent food‑borne illness."},{"claimIndex":14,"stance":"accept","rationale":"Egg dishes should reach an internal temperature of 160 °F for safety."},{"claimIndex":15,"stance":"accept","rationale":"Cooked eggs should not sit at room temperature longer than two hours to avoid bacterial growth."},{"claimIndex":16,"stance":"accept","rationale":"Vegetarian‑fed chickens can develop nutrient deficiencies if their feed lacks proper fortification."},{"claimIndex":17,"stance":"accept","rationale":"There is no solid evidence that a vegetarian diet produces more nutritious eggs than standard feed."},{"claimIndex":18,"stance":"accept","rationale":"Organic eggs come from uncaged hens that have free range within their housing and outdoor access."},{"claimIndex":19,"stance":"accept","rationale":"Brown eggs are not nutritionally superior to white eggs."},{"claimIndex":20,"stance":"accept","rationale":"Egg color and size are determined by the hen’s breed, producing a range of hues and sizes."},{"claimIndex":21,"stance":"accept","rationale":"Yolk color reflects the hen’s feed, not the egg’s nutritional value."},{"claimIndex":22,"stance":"accept","rationale":"‘Large’ eggs are the standard size used in most recipes, though sizes range from medium to jumbo."},{"claimIndex":23,"stance":"accept","rationale":"Egg muffins can be refrigerated for up to five days or frozen for about a month."},{"claimIndex":24,"stance":"accept","rationale":"Chicken eggs dominate the U.S. market, but duck and quail eggs are also consumed worldwide."},{"claimIndex":25,"stance":"accept","rationale":"Eggs provide an affordable source of protein and other nutrients."},{"claimIndex":26,"stance":"accept","rationale":"Eggs are naturally high in cholesterol."},{"claimIndex":27,"stance":"reject","rationale":"Unlike trans and saturated fats, the cholesterol in eggs still raises blood cholesterol and cannot be dismissed."},{"claimIndex":28,"stance":"accept","rationale":"Some studies have indeed found a link between egg consumption and heart disease."},{"claimIndex":29,"stance":"uncertain","rationale":"While accompaniments like bacon may contribute to risk, the exact contribution relative to eggs alone remains unclear."},{"claimIndex":30,"stance":"uncertain","rationale":"Cooking methods can affect cardiovascular risk, but the degree to which they outweigh the egg’s own effect is uncertain."},{"claimIndex":31,"stance":"reject","rationale":"The precautionary view does not endorse up to seven eggs weekly for most healthy people given LDL‑raising evidence."},{"claimIndex":32,"stance":"uncertain","rationale":"Evidence that seven weekly eggs prevent stroke is limited and not conclusive."},{"claimIndex":33,"stance":"uncertain","rationale":"Claims of stroke‑preventing benefits for macular degeneration lack strong supporting data."},{"claimIndex":34,"stance":"accept","rationale":"Research suggests that diabetics eating seven eggs per week face higher heart‑disease risk."},{"claimIndex":35,"stance":"uncertain","rationale":"Conflicting studies on diabetics create uncertainty about any connection."},{"claimIndex":36,"stance":"accept","rationale":"Some research indicates egg consumption may increase the risk of developing diabetes."},{"claimIndex":37,"stance":"accept","rationale":"Current expert guidance recommends keeping dietary cholesterol as low as possible, under 300 mg per day."},{"claimIndex":38,"stance":"accept","rationale":"A large egg contains about 186 mg of cholesterol, all located in the yolk."},{"claimIndex":39,"stance":"uncertain","rationale":"The suggestion that an egg a day is acceptable when other cholesterol sources are low is viewed with caution."},{"claimIndex":40,"stance":"accept","rationale":"Egg whites contain no cholesterol while still providing protein."},{"claimIndex":41,"stance":"accept","rationale":"The PCRM reviewed all egg‑cholesterol studies from 1950 to March 2019."},{"claimIndex":42,"stance":"accept","rationale":"Industry‑funded egg studies rose from 0 % in the 1950s to about 60 % in 2010‑2019."},{"claimIndex":43,"stance":"accept","rationale":"Over 85 % of studies—regardless of funding—showed eggs have unfavorable effects on blood cholesterol."},{"claimIndex":44,"stance":"accept","rationale":"Nearly half of industry‑funded trials reported conclusions discordant with their actual results, versus 13 % of non‑industry trials."},{"claimIndex":45,"stance":"accept","rationale":"In many studies the cholesterol change did not reach statistical significance, leaving a ≥5 % chance it was due to chance."},{"claimIndex":46,"stance":"reject","rationale":"The 2015 Dietary Guidelines claim of no appreciable relationship contradicts the evidence we consider credible."},{"claimIndex":47,"stance":"accept","rationale":"A 2019 meta‑analysis found that one egg per day raises LDL cholesterol by roughly nine points."},{"claimIndex":48,"stance":"accept","rationale":"139 of 153 examined studies reported that eggs raise blood cholesterol."},{"claimIndex":49,"stance":"accept","rationale":"68 of those studies showed statistically significant cholesterol increases."},{"claimIndex":50,"stance":"accept","rationale":"No study reported a significant net decrease in cholesterol concentrations."},{"claimIndex":51,"stance":"accept","rationale":"Six non‑industry‑funded studies observed non‑significant net cholesterol decreases."},{"claimIndex":52,"stance":"accept","rationale":"Eight industry‑funded studies observed non‑significant net cholesterol decreases."},{"claimIndex":53,"stance":"accept","rationale":"The Physicians Committee for Responsible Medicine was founded in 1985 as a nonprofit organization."},{"claimIndex":54,"stance":"uncertain","rationale":"The claim that an egg a day does not increase cardiovascular risk is treated as uncertain given conflicting evidence."},{"claimIndex":55,"stance":"accept","rationale":"A large egg provides about 6 g of protein and 72 kcal."},{"claimIndex":56,"stance":"accept","rationale":"A large egg contains roughly 270 IU of vitamin A and 41 IU of vitamin D."},{"claimIndex":57,"stance":"accept","rationale":"One large egg supplies about 6 g of protein and 72 calories."},{"claimIndex":58,"stance":"uncertain","rationale":"Large cohort studies showing no higher disease rates up to one egg per day are viewed with caution and remain uncertain."},{"claimIndex":59,"stance":"accept","rationale":"Highly refined carbohydrates in white toast, pastries, and similar foods can raise heart‑disease and stroke risk."},{"claimIndex":60,"stance":"accept","rationale":"Approximately one‑third of American adults have high blood pressure."}]}
